example_background: Samantha Elauf applied for a sales associate position at an Abercrombie & Fitch store in Tulsa, Oklahoma. She wore a headscarf to her interview due to her Muslim faith. The store's 'Look Policy' prohibited headwear, and although interviewers gave her a high score, the district manager disallowed her hiring based on the policy. Elauf was never informed of the policy during the process. The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) filed suit on her behalf, alleging failure to accommodate religion during the hiring process.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Supreme Court unanimously ruled in 2015 that Abercrombie & Fitch discriminated against Elauf under Title VII. The Court held that an applicant need only show that their need for accommodation was a motivating factor in the hiring decision, reversing the Tenth Circuit's summary judgment for the company.
source_rule: For the purposes of this policy: “Candidate” includes applicants and candidates who are part of the GitHub application or recruitment process; and
relevant_rules: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964

example_background: The U.S. Department of Labor investigated Oracle Corporation's recruiting practices from 2013 to 2017. Allegations included assigning white male applicants to higher-paying positions, steering female and minority applicants to lower-paid roles, and paying women and minorities less than male counterparts. The investigation covered Oracle's campus recruiting, job advertising, and interview processes, where protected characteristics appeared to influence placement decisions.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the DOL ordered Oracle to pay $50,000 in back wages to affected applicants and implement monitoring and training. Ongoing litigation includes a class-action suit settled in 2017 for similar issues.
source_rule: For the purposes of this policy: “Candidate” includes applicants and candidates who are part of the GitHub application or recruitment process; and
relevant_rules: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act

example_name: Amazon Web Services Subprocessor Disclosure Changes
example_background: Amazon Web Services (AWS) maintains a publicly available list of subprocessors used for its cloud services that handle customer data. The company updates this list on its website and notifies customers of any new subprocessors or changes through email at least 30 days prior to the subprocessor beginning to process customer data. This practice applies to services involving personal data under data protection agreements. AWS provides details on the types of services each subprocessor performs and geographic locations where data processing occurs. Customers have the option to object to new subprocessors within the notice period, allowing AWS to make alternative arrangements if objections are raised. This transparency mechanism has been part of AWS's Data Processing Addendum since at least 2018, with regular updates documented on their compliance pages.
process_and_outcome: AWS continues to operate with this policy, receiving positive feedback in customer trust reports and compliance audits. No enforcement actions have been taken against AWS for subprocessor handling in major jurisdictions.
source_rule: GitHub publishes the names of any new subprocessors for its online services at least 30 days in advance of the subprocessor’s authorization to perform services that may involve access to customer data or personal data.

The company commits to notifying customers of new subprocessors or material changes via email with at least 30 days advance notice before the subprocessor accesses data. This includes information on the subprocessor's location and services provided. Salesforce allows customers to object during the notice period, potentially leading to termination of affected services if no resolution is reached. This process is outlined in their Data Processing Addendum and has been consistently applied across updates to their subprocessor list.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce maintains ongoing compliance, with annual audits and customer notifications ensuring adherence. The policy supports their SOC 2 and GDPR certifications without reported incidents.

example_name: Google €50 Million GDPR Fine by CNIL
example_background: France's data protection authority investigated Google's Android advertising practices, finding that the company processed personal data for personalized ads without fully valid consent. The processing involved identifiers like advertising IDs, location data, and browsing history collected from millions of users without transparent notice or granular consent options.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €50 million fine in 2019, one of the first major GDPR penalties. Google appealed but later enhanced its consent mechanisms. Additional investigations followed in other EU countries.

example_background: Ticketmaster's website placed cookies on users' devices without consent banners, collecting personal data like IP addresses and browsing behavior for analytics and advertising. The practice affected French users and involved pre-checked consent boxes for third-party trackers, processing data without lawful basis.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Ticketmaster €15 million and Live Nation €10 million in 2020. The companies updated their cookie consent mechanisms to comply with GDPR requirements.

example_background: French video-sharing platform Dailymotion was investigated for its data processing practices involving user data uploaded and shared through its services. The platform collected personal data such as IP addresses, device information, and viewing habits from users interacting with videos. The National Commission on Informatics and Liberty (CNIL) found that Dailymotion set approximately 100 cookies on users' devices without proper consent banners or mechanisms, particularly affecting non-subscriber users. This occurred during normal use of the service where users provided data via uploads and interactions.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, CNIL imposed a €150 million fine on Dailymotion's parent company, Vivendi, for cookie-related violations. The regulator noted the lack of user choice in accepting non-essential cookies. Dailymotion updated its consent management platform to comply with requirements.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Children's Data Violations
example_background: Meta Platforms, operating Facebook and Instagram, processed personal data of children under 13 without verifiable parental consent. The platforms allowed account creation using birthdates indicating underage users and collected data such as profile information, interests, and behavioral data through regular use. This involved data provided by minors or on their behalf during sign-up and interactions on the services. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) examined systemic issues in age verification and data handling for young users across the EU.
process_and_outcome: In September 2023, the DPC fined Meta €91 million. Additional investigations led to further scrutiny under GDPR's child data protections. Meta adjusted its age assurance tools and parental consent processes.

process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Amazon France Logistique €32 million in 2024. Amazon discontinued the data collection practice and updated its policies for third-party data handling.

example_background: Cambridge Analytica, a political consulting firm, obtained personal data from millions of Facebook users through an unauthorized app developed by Aleksandr Kogan. The data was collected via Facebook's platform and shared with third parties without user consent or proper transparency. Facebook's policies required approval for data sharing with partners, but the subprocessors handling the data were not adequately disclosed or authorized for such extensive use. This involved data extraction, storage, and analysis by multiple entities, leading to concerns over data protection practices in social media ecosystems.
process_and_outcome: The UK's Information Commissioner's Office conducted an investigation, fining Facebook £500,000 for failing to safeguard user data. Multiple regulatory probes followed, including FTC actions, resulting in a $5 billion settlement by Facebook. Cambridge Analytica ceased operations.

The breach exposed sensitive personal information including Social Security numbers, birth dates, and addresses. Equifax's failure to patch the known vulnerability promptly and inadequate oversight of its cloud subprocessors contributed to the prolonged exposure.

example_background: In 2023, Microsoft, as the owner of GitHub, faced scrutiny over how user data from GitHub repositories was processed and shared. Reports emerged that GitHub's Copilot tool, powered by OpenAI models, had trained on public repositories without explicit opt-out mechanisms initially available to all users. This raised questions about the scope of the GitHub Privacy Statement, which delineates how data from GitHub services is handled separately from other Microsoft privacy policies. Users and developers expressed concerns that code snippets from public repos were incorporated into AI training datasets, potentially leading to reproduction of proprietary or sensitive code in generated outputs. The GitHub Privacy Statement specifies that data usage for GitHub services follows distinct rules, including permissions for public content. This incident highlighted tensions between platform policies and user expectations for data control.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft responded by introducing opt-out options for Copilot training data usage and updated transparency reports. No formal regulatory fines were imposed, but class-action lawsuits were filed in the US, some of which were settled or dismissed. GitHub enhanced its privacy controls and issued detailed blog posts explaining compliance with its Privacy Statement.
source_rule: This policy does not apply to the use of GitHub services, which are covered under our [GitHub Privacy Statement](/site-policy/privacy-policies/github-privacy-statement).

example_background: In 2018, a vulnerability in GitHub's system allowed unauthorized access to private repository data for a short period. The incident involved an attacker exploiting a flaw in the web interface, potentially exposing code and data governed by GitHub's Privacy Statement. This statement outlines that GitHub services handle user data under specific privacy protections distinct from general Microsoft policies, emphasizing security measures for repositories. The breach affected a limited number of users but underscored the importance of segregated privacy rules for GitHub to manage authentication tokens and repository contents securely.
process_and_outcome: GitHub promptly patched the vulnerability, notified affected users, and conducted a full security audit. No data was found to have been exfiltrated, and the company enhanced its bug bounty program. Regulatory bodies like the Irish Data Protection Commission reviewed the matter under GDPR but took no enforcement action.
source_rule: This policy does not apply to the use of GitHub services, which are covered under our [GitHub Privacy Statement](/site-policy/privacy-policies/github-privacy-statement).

example_name: Union Pacific Railroad GitHub Secrets Exposure
example_background: In 2023, security researcher Arsenii Kostromin discovered over 4,000 GitHub repositories containing exposed secrets, including private keys from Union Pacific Railroad. These included AWS keys and other credentials inadvertently committed to public or private repos not properly secured. GitHub's Privacy Statement covers how such data in repositories is processed, noting that it does not apply to broader Microsoft services, and relies on user responsibility for secret management. The exposure risked access to internal systems, highlighting gaps in applying platform-specific privacy and security practices.
process_and_outcome: GitHub's secret scanning tool flagged some but not all instances. Union Pacific revoked compromised keys, and the incident was publicly disclosed via research reports. No major breach occurred, but it prompted industry-wide improvements in secret detection.
source_rule: This policy does not apply to the use of GitHub services, which are covered under our [GitHub Privacy Statement](/site-policy/privacy-policies/github-privacy-statement).

example_background: GitHub Enterprise customers in the EU underwent GDPR compliance reviews in 2020-2021, focusing on data residency and processing under the GitHub Privacy Statement. The statement clarifies that GitHub services use distinct data handling practices, including EU data centers for Enterprise users to ensure data stays within the region. Several large enterprises tested configurations to align with data protection requirements, demonstrating how the policy enables compliance through features like private instances and audit logs.
process_and_outcome: Audits confirmed adherence, with GitHub providing Data Processing Agreements (DPAs). Customers achieved GDPR certification without issues, and GitHub published case studies on successful implementations.
source_rule: This policy does not apply to the use of GitHub services, which are covered under our [GitHub Privacy Statement](/site-policy/privacy-policies/github-privacy-statement).

example_name: Codecov Bash Uploader Supply Chain Incident on GitHub
example_background: In 2021, the Codecov bash uploader script used in CI/CD pipelines on GitHub was modified by an attacker to exfiltrate environment variables from users' machines. This affected numerous GitHub Actions workflows, where secrets and env vars were exposed. GitHub's Privacy Statement governs data in services like Actions, separating it from other Microsoft policies, and emphasizes user-configured security. The incident involved over 1,000 organizations, including Apple and Uber, revealing risks in third-party integrations within GitHub ecosystems.
process_and_outcome: Codecov and GitHub investigated, rotated affected credentials, and enhanced supply chain security features like dependency scanning. No fines, but it led to GitHub's Dependabot improvements and industry alerts.
source_rule: This policy does not apply to the use of GitHub services, which are covered under our [GitHub Privacy Statement](/site-policy/privacy-policies/github-privacy-statement).

example_name: GitHub Advanced Security SOC2 Type II Certification
example_background: In 2022, GitHub achieved SOC 2 Type II attestation for its Advanced Security features, validating controls over data handling in GitHub services as per its Privacy Statement. This involved rigorous audits of code scanning, secret scanning, and dependency review, ensuring that customer data processed on the platform meets trust services criteria. The Privacy Statement's separation allows tailored security for GitHub, supporting enterprise adoption without broader Microsoft policy constraints.
process_and_outcome: Independent auditors confirmed effective controls over 12 months. GitHub published the report, enabling customers to meet their own compliance needs.

GitHub's Privacy Statement covers public repo content, which was scanned post-incident. example_name: LinkedIn Data Breach and GDPR Fine
example_background: In 2020, LinkedIn, a professional networking platform owned by Microsoft, was fined €310,000 by the Dutch Data Protection Authority (AP) for mishandling personal data of Dutch users. The issue stemmed from LinkedIn's data processing practices for advertising and profiling without explicit consent, affecting millions of users who provided profile information including names, job titles, skills, and contact details for career-related purposes. The investigation revealed that LinkedIn processed data of non-members based on member interactions, using it for targeted ads without proper legal basis.
process_and_outcome: Following a complaint from a privacy advocacy group, the Dutch AP investigated and issued the fine in December 2020. LinkedIn appealed but adjusted its practices to align with GDPR requirements, including clearer consent mechanisms for data use in recruitment and advertising.
source_rule: "Candidate Personal Information" refers to any information we collect about one of our Candidates during the application or recruitment process which could, alone or together with other information, personally identify them or otherwise be reasonably linked or connected with them.

example_background: SAP SuccessFactors, a cloud-based HR software platform used for recruitment and applicant tracking, implements strict data minimization and pseudonymization for candidate personal information including resumes, contact details, and qualifications. The platform provides configurable privacy settings, audit logs, and compliance dashboards to ensure data is only processed with consent and retained as necessary, serving thousands of enterprises globally.
process_and_outcome: SAP has maintained GDPR adequacy decisions and SOC 2 compliance certifications, with no major enforcement actions. Clients report streamlined compliance during audits.
source_rule: "Candidate Personal Information" refers to any information we collect about one of our Candidates during the application or recruitment process which could, alone or together with other information, personally identify them or otherwise be reasonably linked or connected with them.

example_background: TikTok Ireland Ltd faced scrutiny over data processing practices involving third-party subprocessors for user data analytics and content recommendation features. The Irish Data Protection Commission examined notifications to users and contractual safeguards with these subprocessors during an investigation into broader data handling practices.
process_and_outcome: The investigation resulted in a 345 million euro fine imposed on TikTok for violations related to child data processing and insufficient transparency, with specific findings on subprocessors contributing to the lack of adequate information provided to data subjects. TikTok was ordered to implement changes to its subprocessor management.

example_background: In 2015, The Walt Disney Company restructured its IT department in Florida, leading to the layoff of approximately 250 American workers. The company required the departing employees to train their foreign replacements who had been brought to the United States on H-1B visas. These replacements were hired through a staffing firm, and the process involved Disney covering visa-related costs and facilitating the transition. The action drew widespread attention as affected workers were instructed to document the training provided to the incoming H-1B visa holders, raising concerns about the use of the visa program to displace domestic labor. This occurred amid broader discussions on H-1B visa practices in the tech sector.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Department of Labor investigated the circumstances, and while no formal penalties were imposed on Disney, the company settled related claims and faced congressional scrutiny. Legislation was proposed to address similar practices, and the incident contributed to reforms in H-1B oversight.
source_rule: * If you are offered a position and where requested by you, to assist you with obtaining an immigration visa or work permit;
involved_parties: H-1B visa holders via staffing firms

example_background: Infosys, a major Indian IT consulting firm, engaged in a pattern of visa fraud between 2013 and 2019 by misrepresenting the true job locations and client details on hundreds of H-1B and B-1 visa petitions. The company filed petitions claiming employees would work at specific U.S. locations but then placed them at different client sites without updating authorities. Infosys also used B-1 business visas for actual paid work, which is prohibited. This allowed the firm to bring in workers for extended periods while avoiding standard H-1B caps and fees, affecting thousands of petitions and enabling cost savings on labor.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Department of Justice prosecuted the case, resulting in a $34 million criminal penalty paid by Infosys in 2018, the largest visa fraud settlement at the time. The company entered a deferred prosecution agreement and implemented compliance reforms.
source_rule: * If you are offered a position and where requested by you, to assist you with obtaining an immigration visa or work permit;

example_background: In 2023, the U.S. Department of Labor fined Google for shortcomings in its PERM labor certification process required for H-1B visas. During recruitment for permanent positions, Google excluded U.S. workers by using overly restrictive criteria, such as requiring niche experience levels or specific educational backgrounds that deterred domestic applicants. The investigation covered multiple job postings where the company failed to adequately test the labor market, potentially facilitating easier approval of foreign worker petitions.
process_and_outcome: Google paid $175,000 in back wages to 2 U.S. workers and a civil penalty. The settlement highlighted deficiencies in recruitment documentation and advertising practices.
source_rule: * If you are offered a position and where requested by you, to assist you with obtaining an immigration visa or work permit;

example_name: Clarian Health Partners H-1B Fraud Conviction
example_background: Clarian Health Partners (now Indiana University Health) was involved in a scheme where executives approved fake job offers and exaggerated salaries on H-1B petitions for foreign nurses. From 2001 to 2006, the hospital sponsored visas for nurses who never worked the listed positions, using the program to address staffing shortages deceptively.
process_and_outcome: Two executives were convicted on visa fraud charges, receiving prison sentences and fines totaling over $1 million. The organization paid restitution and reformed hiring practices.

example_background: In 2014, an app called 'thisisyourdigitallife' developed by Aleksandr Kogan collected data from approximately 270,000 Facebook users who consented to a personality quiz. The app also accessed data from the Facebook friend networks of those users, totaling data on up to 87 million individuals including names, emails, phone numbers, locations, and profile information. This data was shared with Cambridge Analytica, a political consulting firm, which used it for targeted advertising in political campaigns such as the 2016 US presidential election and Brexit referendum. Facebook's platform policies required that user data, including contact information from friends, be deleted after app use and not transferred to third parties without explicit permission. The incident came to light in 2018 through investigative journalism by The Guardian and The New York Times, revealing how the data was harvested without the knowledge or consent of the vast majority of affected individuals whose friend connections were scraped.
process_and_outcome: The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated and fined Facebook £500,000 in 2018 for failing to protect user data. The US Federal Trade Commission (FTC) settled with Facebook for $5 billion in 2019 over privacy violations. Cambridge Analytica ceased operations and filed for bankruptcy in 2018. Facebook faced multiple class-action lawsuits and congressional hearings, leading to policy changes including stricter app data access rules.
source_rule: **Identification and contact information**, including your name, email address, phone number, location, GitHub profile, and any other information you provide (such as referrals and references).
relevant_rules: Facebook Platform Policy on User Data
FTC Act Section 5 on Unfair Practices

example_name: FTC Enforcement Against GoodRx for Location and Health Data Sharing
example_background: GoodRx, a digital healthcare platform providing prescription discount services, collected and disclosed users' personal health data including prescription records, locations, IP addresses, device IDs, and contact information to third-party advertisers like Google and Facebook from 2017 to 2020. The company tracked users' visits to pharmacy websites and shared detailed health queries and geolocation data (accurate to store level) without user consent or awareness. This occurred through pixel trackers embedded on GoodRx pages, allowing ad networks to build profiles on sensitive medical information tied to identifiers like email addresses obtained during sign-ups.
process_and_outcome: In February 2023, the FTC prohibited GoodRx from sharing health data for advertising and required annual audits, privacy program certification, and 20 years of compliance reporting. No monetary penalty was imposed, but the action set a precedent for health data privacy.
source_rule: **Identification and contact information**, including your name, email address, phone number, location, GitHub profile, and any other information you provide (such as referrals and references).

example_name: Apple's App Tracking Transparency (ATT) Framework Implementation
example_background: In 2021, Apple introduced App Tracking Transparency (ATT), a feature in iOS 14.5 requiring apps to obtain explicit user permission before tracking them across apps and websites using the Identifier for Advertisers (IDFA). This included access to device identifiers linked to contact information like email and phone numbers collected via sign-ins. The policy shift aimed to protect user privacy by prompting users with a consent dialog whenever an app attempted to request tracking authorization, significantly reducing unauthorized sharing of personal identifiers with ad networks.
process_and_outcome: The implementation led to widespread adoption, with studies showing over 80% of users opting out of tracking. Apple faced no penalties and received praise from privacy advocates, while ad industry revenues adjusted to privacy-respecting alternatives.
source_rule: **Identification and contact information**, including your name, email address, phone number, location, GitHub profile, and any other information you provide (such as referrals and references).

example_name: GitHub Copilot Data Privacy Compliance Audit
example_background: GitHub, owned by Microsoft, launched GitHub Copilot in 2021, an AI-powered code completion tool trained on public repositories. Concerns arose about potential exposure of personal data like contact information embedded in code comments or profiles. GitHub implemented strict policies requiring users to provide accurate identification and contact details for account creation, while ensuring Copilot does not retain or share private repository data containing such information. Regular third-party audits verified compliance with data minimization principles.
process_and_outcome: Independent audits by firms like Schellman confirmed compliance with privacy standards. No major incidents or fines occurred, positioning GitHub as a model for AI tool data handling in developer platforms.
source_rule: **Identification and contact information**, including your name, email address, phone number, location, GitHub profile, and any other information you provide (such as referrals and references).

example_background: In October 2020, the Hamburg Data Protection Authority fined H&M 35.3 million euros for violations related to employee data handling. H&M's German subsidiary systematically collected and stored detailed notes on approximately 1,000 employees' private lives, such as vacation stories, illnesses, religious beliefs, family issues, pregnancies, and personal problems. This data was gathered during so-called 'welcome back talks' after absences and stored in shared drives accessible to up to 50 managers. The information was used to assess employee performance, reliability, and suitability for promotions or new roles, effectively treating employee personal data as part of recruitment and internal mobility processes. Employees were not informed about the extent of data collection, and the data was retained indefinitely without justification.
process_and_outcome: Following employee complaints, the Hamburg DPA investigated and determined the practices violated GDPR principles of lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, data minimization, and storage limitation. H&M was fined 35,258,707 euros, the first major GDPR penalty for employee data misuse.
source_rule: ## How do we use the Candidate Personal Information we collect?We use the Candidate Personal Information for the following purposes: * To assess your skills, qualifications and interests for employment opportunities with GitHub; * To verify the information provided by you or others, including checking your references;  * To communicate with you about your application and the recruitment process, including informing you of other potential employment opportunities at GitHub;

example_background: In December 2022, the French data protection authority CNIL fined LinkedIn 310 million euros for misuse of personal data in advertising. LinkedIn processed user data—including professional profiles, skills, and employment history collected during job applications and networking—for targeted ads without valid consent. Data from non-users was also processed via cookies for behavioral advertising during job searches. This involved using candidate information beyond initial recruitment communications, such as for unrelated promotional purposes, without clear transparency or opt-in mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: CNIL's investigation followed online complaints about cookies and advertising practices. The fine was issued for lack of consent under GDPR Article 6(1)(a), insufficient information under Articles 13-14, and violations of purpose limitation.

process_and_outcome: Sephora entered into an Assurance of Compliance, agreeing to update privacy notices, implement opt-out tools, and train employees. No monetary penalty was imposed, but it served as an early enforcement example.

example_name: Uber Data Breach Third-Party Vendor Incident 2016
example_background: In October 2016, Uber experienced a data breach where hackers accessed personal information of 57 million users and drivers through GitHub repositories managed by a third-party contractor. Uber's private keys and credentials were inadvertently stored in a publicly accessible GitHub repository, which was discovered and exploited by attackers. This repository was used by an external contractor for development purposes, and the incident highlighted issues with third-party access to sensitive data, including driver names, email addresses, phone numbers, and license numbers. Uber paid the hackers $100,000 to delete the data and did not disclose the breach initially, affecting processes like background checks for drivers where such personal information is shared with third parties.
process_and_outcome: Investigations by the FTC, UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO), and others led to Uber paying $148 million to all 50 U.S. states and the District of Columbia. The ICO fined Uber £385,000 under GDPR precursors. Uber implemented improved third-party access controls and breach notification protocols.
source_rule: 1. In order to carry out the uses of Candidate Personal Information described above; 1. To enable third parties to provide products or services to us or on our behalf (such as to facilitate the application process or conduct background checks).

example_background: Marriott International acquired Starwood Hotels in 2016, inheriting a database containing personal data of about 383 million guests, including names, passport numbers, travel details, and payment information. A third-party vulnerability in the Starwood reservation system, exploited since 2014, allowed unauthorized access until detected in 2018. Marriott used third-party service providers for IT infrastructure and booking systems, and the delay in securing the acquired system after the merger exposed candidate-like personal data used in hospitality services, analogous to application facilitation. The ICO noted deficiencies in third-party vendor security assessments post-acquisition.
process_and_outcome: The UK's ICO issued a £99 million fine in 2020 under GDPR for failing to secure personal data and inadequate vendor oversight. Marriott also faced a €18.4 million fine from Spain's data protection authority. The company enhanced its data protection program, including third-party audits.

example_name: SEC Enforcement Against SolarWinds Third-Party Supply Chain Attack 2023
example_background: In December 2020, nation-state actors compromised SolarWinds' Orion software update mechanism, affecting 18,000 customers including U.S. government agencies. While SolarWinds was the primary target, the SEC charged the company in 2023 for misleading disclosures about cybersecurity risks related to third-party software components used in their products. Customers like Microsoft and FireEye used SolarWinds for IT management, sharing personal employee data for access controls, and the breach potentially exposed such information indirectly through supply chain dependencies.
process_and_outcome: The SEC settled with SolarWinds for $26 million and its CISO for $1 million without admitting wrongdoing. The company agreed to cease-and-desist orders and implement stronger disclosure controls for third-party risks.

example_name: CPRA Enforcement Against Sephora Third-Party Sharing 2022
example_background: Sephora, a cosmetics retailer, shared personal information of California consumers with third-party services like analytics providers and advertising partners without providing required opt-out mechanisms under the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA). The data included identifiers and preferences collected via website interactions, similar to candidate data shared for services. Sephora used these third parties to enhance customer experiences, but failed to honor 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' requests properly.
process_and_outcome: The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) settled with Sephora for $1.2 million in July 2022, the first CPRA enforcement action. Sephora agreed to update its privacy practices, implement opt-out tools, and conduct regular compliance audits for third-party sharing.
source_rule: 1. In order to carry out the uses of Candidate Personal Information described above; 1. To enable third parties to provide products or services to us or on our behalf (such as to facilitate the application process or conduct background checks).

example_name: FTC Action Against GoodRx Third-Party Data Sharing 2023
example_background: GoodRx, a prescription discount platform, publicly advertised not sharing personal health data but disclosed sensitive user information, including prescription records and health conditions, to third-party advertisers like Google and Facebook via tracking technologies like pixels. This data was shared without user consent for advertising services, despite privacy policy claims, affecting millions of users whose health-related personal information was treated similarly to candidate data in service facilitation.
process_and_outcome: The FTC imposed a $1.5 million civil penalty and comprehensive restrictions on GoodRx's data practices, including bans on sharing health data with advertisers and requirements for privacy assessments before third-party sharing.
source_rule: 1. In order to carry out the uses of Candidate Personal Information described above; 1. To enable third parties to provide products or services to us or on our behalf (such as to facilitate the application process or conduct background checks).

example_background: Automatic Data Processing (ADP), a major payroll and HR services provider, suffered a breach in 2013 where a third-party contractor's credentials were used to access client data, including Social Security numbers and bank details of employees. The contractor had remote access for payroll processing services, and weak access controls allowed the breach to expose data used in background checks and application processes facilitated through ADP's platform.
process_and_outcome: ADP notified affected parties and settled class-action lawsuits for millions. It enhanced third-party vendor security protocols, including multi-factor authentication and regular audits, without major regulatory fines but under FTC scrutiny.
source_rule: 1. In order to carry out the uses of Candidate Personal Information described above; 1. To enable third parties to provide products or services to us or on our behalf (such as to facilitate the application process or conduct background checks).

example_name: Salesforce Data Cloud Compliance Best Practice
example_background: Salesforce implemented its Data Cloud platform with built-in controls for sharing candidate personal information with third-party services like background check providers and application facilitators. The platform uses zero-trity architecture, customer-managed keys, and granular permissions to ensure third parties only access necessary data for specified services, such as during hiring processes on platforms like GitHub Jobs or integrated ATS systems.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce achieved SOC 2 Type II compliance and ISO 27001 certification, demonstrating effective third-party data handling. No major breaches reported, and it serves as an industry benchmark audited annually.

example_background: Workday, an enterprise cloud platform for HR and finance, enables third-party services for background checks and application processing through its secure integration framework. It enforces data minimization, encryption in transit and at rest, and vendor risk assessments before granting access to candidate personal information, aligning with hiring workflows.
process_and_outcome: Workday maintains continuous compliance monitoring, passing multiple third-party audits with no enforcement actions. It publishes transparency reports on data handling practices.
source_rule: 1. In order to carry out the uses of Candidate Personal Information described above; 1. To enable third parties to provide products or services to us or on our behalf (such as to facilitate the application process or conduct background checks).

example_background: Freeman Companies, an event services provider, faced allegations regarding its pre-employment background check process. The company requested criminal history information from job applicants early in the hiring process, which disproportionately affected minority candidates. Applicants provided employment history, education details, and national identifiers through applications. The policy was applied uniformly but resulted in disparate impact, as it deterred protected class members from proceeding without individualized assessment.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) sued in 2014. The court ruled in favor of the EEOC, finding disparate impact violation. Freeman settled for $3.75 million, including back pay and compensation for 327 class members, and revised its hiring policies to delay criminal checks and use targeted criteria.
source_rule: **National identifiers**, such as your citizenship status, residency and work permit status. **Employment and education history**, such as your resume or CV, cover letter, details of how you heard about the position you are applying for, information about any previous employment at GitHub or affiliates of GitHub, or other information you provide to us in support of an application and/or the application and recruitment process.
relevant_rules: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964

example_background: LinkedIn, a professional networking platform owned by Microsoft, collects employment history, education details, and inferred residency information from user profiles and job applications. Following GDPR implementation, LinkedIn updated its data processing practices to include granular consent options, data portability tools, and automated deletion of inactive profiles. For recruitment, it ensures that applicant-provided national identifiers and CV data are processed only with consent, stored securely, and not shared without permission, serving as a model for handling such data at scale.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission reviewed LinkedIn's practices without issuing fines for recruitment data handling. LinkedIn published compliance reports and enhanced features like 'Data Privacy Controls,' achieving sustained adherence through third-party certifications.
source_rule: **National identifiers**, such as your citizenship status, residency and work permit status. **Employment and education history**, such as your resume or CV, cover letter, details of how you heard about the position you are applying for, information about any previous employment at GitHub or affiliates of GitHub, or other information you provide to us in support of an application and/or the application and recruitment process.

example_name: Google Privacy Sandbox for Recruitment Data
example_background: Google developed the Privacy Sandbox initiative to replace third-party cookies with privacy-preserving alternatives, applied to its recruitment tools like Google for Jobs. This ensures that employment history, education, and location-based residency data shared in job searches are processed without unique identifiers, using aggregated and anonymized signals. Google's applicant tracking systems emphasize consent and minimal data collection for features involving national and professional background information.
process_and_outcome: Google collaborated with regulators like the U.K. CMA and received approval for Privacy Sandbox trials. No enforcement actions for HR data handling, with ongoing compliance validated through transparency reports and industry audits.
source_rule: **National identifiers**, such as your citizenship status, residency and work permit status. **Employment and education history**, such as your resume or CV, cover letter, details of how you heard about the position you are applying for, information about any previous employment at GitHub or affiliates of GitHub, or other information you provide to us in support of an application and/or the application and recruitment process.

example_background: In 2017, Equifax experienced a data breach affecting 147 million consumers, exposing personal information including names, Social Security numbers, birth dates, and addresses. The company faced obligations to notify affected individuals and regulators as required by data protection laws. Equifax prepared and submitted detailed reports to government authorities detailing the breach scope, timeline, and mitigation steps. The incident occurred due to unpatched software vulnerabilities in the Apache Struts web application framework, which the company failed to address promptly despite available patches. Following the breach, Equifax engaged in extensive remediation, including system upgrades and consumer notification processes. The event highlighted the importance of timely compliance with breach notification mandates to manage public trust and legal requirements.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Trade Commission, Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, and multiple state attorneys general investigated. Equifax agreed to a settlement exceeding $700 million, including $425 million for consumer compensation, $100 million civil penalty to the CFPB, and $100 million to states. The company also committed to enhanced data security practices for 20 years under FTC oversight.
source_rule: * To comply with local laws, regulations, legal processes or enforceable government requests; and * To prepare and submit reports as required under local laws and regulations;  * To manage and improve our application and recruitment process (such as making the application process more efficient and improving our diversity practices).

example_background: In 2021, data from approximately 700 million LinkedIn users, including profile information such as names, email addresses, phone numbers, workplace details, and gender, appeared on a hacker forum. The data was scraped using publicly available APIs rather than a traditional breach. LinkedIn investigated and determined no internal systems were compromised. The company notified affected users and regulators about the scraping incident, emphasizing that while data was public, it was aggregated and shared without authorization. LinkedIn's practices involved rate limiting and terms of service to manage data access during recruitment and networking processes.
process_and_outcome: No formal enforcement actions resulted as regulators accepted LinkedIn's assessment of public data exposure. The company implemented enhanced API restrictions and monitoring to prevent future scraping.
source_rule: * To comply with local laws, regulations, legal processes or enforceable government requests; and * To prepare and submit reports as required under local laws and regulations;  * To manage and improve our application and recruitment process (such as making the application process more efficient and improving our diversity practices).

example_background: Upon the enforcement of GDPR in May 2018, Google implemented extensive measures to handle data processing for its services, including recruitment tools. The company established processes to respond to regulatory requests from EU data protection authorities, submit annual transparency reports, and manage applicant data efficiently. This included automating parts of the hiring process while ensuring compliance with data minimization and consent requirements. Google faced scrutiny over ad targeting but demonstrated structured reporting and legal process adherence.
process_and_outcome: Google submitted required GDPR documentation and faced investigations but achieved compliance certifications. CNIL issued fines for specific ad practices totaling 150 million euros, but recruitment processes were upheld as compliant.
source_rule: * To comply with local laws, regulations, legal processes or enforceable government requests; and * To prepare and submit reports as required under local laws and regulations;  * To manage and improve our application and recruitment process (such as making the application process more efficient and improving our diversity practices).

example_background: China's ride-hailing company Didi collected excessive user data including facial recognition scans and precise location information, transferring it to U.S. servers without proper approvals. Didi failed to submit required cybersecurity reports to Chinese regulators during its U.S. IPO process in 2021. The company managed recruitment data similarly without full compliance disclosures.
process_and_outcome: The Cyberspace Administration of China ordered Didi to cease new user registration and imposed a 1.2 billion yuan fine. Didi delisted from NYSE and restructured operations.
source_rule: * To comply with local laws, regulations, legal processes or enforceable government requests; and * To prepare and submit reports as required under local laws and regulations;  * To manage and improve our application and recruitment process (such as making the application process more efficient and improving our diversity practices).

example_background: Apple updated its privacy policies and developer agreements to comply with GDPR by implementing Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) for data transfers outside the EU. This included handling professional information from developer profiles, App Store submissions, and public resources like LinkedIn-linked accounts used for verification. Apple established data processing agreements ensuring that employment details, salary negotiation indicators from job postings, and background check data were protected with explicit consent mechanisms and minimized collection. The company conducted Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) for high-risk processing of such information.
process_and_outcome: Apple's practices passed multiple regulatory audits with no major fines. The company was praised in GDPR enforcement reports for proactive compliance, including transparency reports detailing data handling for professional information.
source_rule: **Professional or employment information**, including your desired salary or terms related to benefits, willingness to relocate, other job preferences, interview details, reference information and/or information received from background checks (where applicable), and information from publicly available resources (such as your LinkedIn profile or website).

example_name: SEC Charges Against Oracle for Background Check Disclosures
example_background: Oracle's HR subsidiary, Oracle America, maintained a database of job applicant and employee records including background check results, reference information, salary negotiation notes, and relocation willingness scraped or collected from public profiles and submissions. Inaccurate reporting to the SEC about data breach incidents involving this sensitive employment data led to disclosure failures. The database handled details from over 5 million records, integrating public LinkedIn data with private HR notes.
process_and_outcome: The SEC charged Oracle with misleading disclosures, resulting in a $10 million civil penalty in 2022. Oracle neither admitted nor denied findings and enhanced disclosure controls.
source_rule: **Professional or employment information**, including your desired salary or terms related to benefits, willingness to relocate, other job preferences, interview details, reference information and/or information received from background checks (where applicable), and information from publicly available resources (such as your LinkedIn profile or website).

example_name: Uber Data Breach Involving Employee Records
example_background: In 2016, hackers accessed Uber's AWS S3 bucket containing data from 57 million users and 600,000 drivers, including some internal HR files with employee professional details like salary bands, performance reviews, and background check summaries. Publicly sourced data from driver profiles mimicking LinkedIn-style professional info was also exposed. Uber paid hackers $100,000 to delete the data and concealed the breach from regulators for a year.
process_and_outcome: FTC and state AGs settled for $148 million in 2018. UK ICO fined Uber £385,000. Criminal charges against former CSO and employees dropped after cooperation.
source_rule: **Professional or employment information**, including your desired salary or terms related to benefits, willingness to relocate, other job preferences, interview details, reference information and/or information received from background checks (where applicable), and information from publicly available resources (such as your LinkedIn profile or website).

example_background: In 2012, Target Corporation's analytics team developed algorithms to predict customer pregnancies based on purchasing patterns, such as unscented lotion and supplements, often correlating with demographic and health-related behaviors. The system assigned 'pregnancy prediction scores' and sent targeted coupons. When a Minnesota father received baby product ads for his teenage daughter, who was secretly pregnant, it exposed how retailers infer sensitive health information from shopping data without explicit disclosure, raising privacy issues around unsolicited demographic profiling.
process_and_outcome: No formal regulatory fine, but the incident drew FTC scrutiny and congressional inquiries into data broker practices. Target adjusted marketing to mix baby ads with neutral ones. It highlighted retail analytics risks, influencing later privacy laws.
source_rule: **Sensitive or demographic information**, such as your gender, medical or health information, veteran status, or your racial or ethnic origin.
relevant_rules: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) inferences

example_background: In October and November 2016, hackers accessed Uber's GitHub repository containing sensitive data of over 57 million Uber users and drivers worldwide, including names, email addresses, phone numbers, and in some cases locations and license numbers. The data was stored in an unsecured private GitHub repository that was inadvertently made public. Uber paid the hackers $100,000 to delete the data and did not disclose the breach publicly for over a year, classifying it as a 'bug bounty' issue. This delay occurred despite the data being used for internal verification processes, including driver background checks required for platform operations. The incident involved failure to promptly inform affected parties whose personal information was part of background screening datasets.
process_and_outcome: Investigations by the FTC, New York Attorney General, and others led to Uber paying $148 million in settlements, including $20 million to the FTC and $24.55 million to all 50 U.S. states and the District of Columbia. Uber's then-CSO and Chief Legal Officer resigned, and the company implemented a comprehensive data security program with third-party audits.
source_rule: * If you were referred, to inform the referrer of the status of your application;  * If you are offered a position, to prepare your offer letter and conduct a background check (to the extent permitted by applicable law);

example_background: Insight Services, a background check company, provided consumer reports to landlords for tenant screening without taking reasonable measures to ensure accuracy. The company used outdated public records databases containing errors, such as criminal convictions that were expunged or belonged to other individuals with similar names. Reports included inaccurate eviction and criminal history data, affecting consumers' housing opportunities. Insight failed to maintain strict procedures to verify data before including it in reports, despite FCRA requirements for maximum possible accuracy in background screening for employment and other purposes.
process_and_outcome: The FTC settled with Insight Services and its owner, requiring cessation of FCRA violations, implementation of a comprehensive accuracy program, deletion of unverified data, and $1 million in redress (suspended due to inability to pay). The company was monitored for 20 years.
source_rule: * If you were referred, to inform the referrer of the status of your application;  * If you are offered a position, to prepare your offer letter and conduct a background check (to the extent permitted by applicable law);

example_background: GoodHire, a background check service provider, developed processes to handle applicant data securely, including obtaining permissible purpose consent, providing pre-adverse action notices, and conducting FCRA-compliant background checks for employers. When applicants were offered positions contingent on checks, GoodHire ensured notifications and summaries of rights were sent promptly. The company maintains SOC 2 Type II compliance and integrates with applicant tracking systems to inform referrers of status updates without violating privacy.
process_and_outcome: GoodHire received FCRA certification from the FTC and maintains an exemplary record with no major enforcement actions. It serves thousands of employers with compliant screening, demonstrating effective data handling for offer-related background checks.
source_rule: * If you were referred, to inform the referrer of the status of your application;  * If you are offered a position, to prepare your offer letter and conduct a background check (to the extent permitted by applicable law);

example_name: LinkedIn FCRA Compliance Settlement with FTC
example_background: LinkedIn sent over 700,000 emails between 2011 and 2015 urging non-members to 'confirm your invite' or 'confirm your connection,' mimicking responses to invitations. These emails included tracking pixels and did not clearly disclose they were advertisements. While not directly employment screening, LinkedIn's platform data is used for reference checks and recruiter background verification, and the case underscores notification accuracy in user status updates.
process_and_outcome: The FTC alleged deception under Section 5. LinkedIn settled for $13 million in refunds to affected consumers and agreed to clear disclosures in future emails.

example_background: Checkr, a modern background check company, built its platform to comply with FCRA by automating notifications to applicants about report status, providing copies of reports before adverse actions, and summarizing rights. For job offers, Checkr conducts checks and informs employers (referrers) of completion status securely. It handles millions of checks annually with built-in consent flows and data protection.
process_and_outcome: Checkr achieved FCRA compliance certification and partnerships with major platforms like Uber and DoorDash without enforcement issues. It invests in AI for accuracy and transparency in reporting.

example_background: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) serves HR tech companies and recruitment firms that upload candidate data for processing. Google lists all subprocessors on its website, requires customer approval for changes, and ensures data sharing with affiliates and vendors adheres to strict contracts mirroring customer privacy policies. For candidate personal information used in AI-driven recruitment tools like Google for Jobs, data access is restricted to purposes such as matching and analytics, with encryption and access controls in place. This model was tested during EU GDPR enforcement waves.
process_and_outcome: EU data protection authorities conducted compliance checks on Google Cloud customers with no enforcement actions against Google's subprocessing practices. Google maintained a clean record in transparency reports and supported customers in achieving compliance certifications like SOC 2 Type II.

example_background: Workday, a cloud-based HR and recruitment platform, shares candidate personal information with affiliates and vetted third-party vendors for features like background checks, payroll integration, and AI talent sourcing. Its master service agreements specify that subprocessors must meet equivalent security standards, undergo annual audits, and use data only for contracted purposes. Workday provides customers with subprocessor lists and objection rights, ensuring transparency in sharing for legitimate business needs such as onboarding and compliance reporting.
process_and_outcome: Workday completed GDPR adequacy decisions and CCPA compliance validations without fines. Customer audits and third-party certifications confirmed effective controls, with no reported incidents of unauthorized third-party data use.

example_background: Automatic Data Processing (ADP), a payroll and HR services provider, experienced a breach in 2019 affecting 18,000 customers when attackers accessed a support portal using stolen credentials. This exposed sensitive employee and candidate data including Social Security numbers and bank details shared with ADP's affiliates and vendors for payroll processing. The credentials were compromised via phishing, and multi-factor authentication was not enforced on the legacy portal used by third-party support staff.
process_and_outcome: ADP notified affected parties and offered credit monitoring. No regulatory fines were imposed, but the company faced lawsuits settled out of court. ADP rolled out mandatory MFA across all portals and enhanced vendor access controls, reporting improvements in annual security assessments.

example_background: Apple has a structured process for handling government requests for customer data from its iCloud services. In its transparency reports, Apple discloses receiving thousands of warrants and subpoenas annually from U.S. law enforcement agencies seeking access to user data such as emails, photos, and location information stored on U.S. servers. Apple reviews each request for legal validity before providing data, notifying users when permitted by law, and publishing aggregate statistics on requests fulfilled. For instance, in the second half of 2022, Apple received 12,269 U.S. legal demands and produced data in 81% of cases after validation.
process_and_outcome: Apple complied with valid warrants by providing requested data within legal timelines, while challenging invalid or overly broad requests in court. No major penalties were imposed, and the company maintained transparency through biannual reports detailing compliance rates and pushback efforts.

To comply with our legal obligations, regulations or contracts, or to respond to a court order, administrative or judicial process (such as subpoena, government audit or search warrant) or, in response to lawful requests by public authorities (such as national security or law enforcement);

example_background: U.S. authorities issued subpoenas to Google for Gmail user data in criminal investigations. Google, as a provider of electronic communication services, received requests for content and non-content data such as account logs and IP addresses. In one documented instance involving a drug trafficking probe, Google was compelled to produce emails and metadata after court review. Google's transparency reports from 2023 indicate receiving over 40,000 U.S. legal demands in the first half of the year, with data production in approximately 70-80% of validated cases. The company notifies users unless gagged by court order.
process_and_outcome: Google complied by producing data for approved requests, cooperating fully with authorities while publishing denial rates for improper demands. Legal proceedings resulted in data disclosure without further escalation or fines for Google.

example_background: In 2007, Yahoo received a National Security Letter (NSL) from the FBI demanding customer data and a gag order preventing disclosure. The NSL sought information on a specific account for a national security investigation. Yahoo initially complied but later joined lawsuits challenging the constitutionality of NSL gag orders, arguing they infringed on First Amendment rights. This was part of broader efforts by tech companies to limit overly broad government data demands without judicial oversight.
process_and_outcome: Yahoo provided the requested data per the NSL. Subsequent court rulings partially upheld challenges to gag orders, leading to reforms allowing limited disclosure. No penalties were assessed against Yahoo for compliance.

In transparency reports for the first half of 2023, Meta reported 15,928 U.S. legal demands, complying with 82% after legal review. A notable example involved warrants in the January 6 Capitol riot investigations, where Meta provided account data from thousands of users to assist federal probes into participants.
process_and_outcome: Meta disclosed data in response to valid warrants, aiding investigations that led to numerous indictments. The company faced no enforcement actions for its handling and continued biannual reporting.

example_background: Revealed by Edward Snowden in 2013, AT&T participated in the NSA's PRISM program, providing bulk metadata from phone calls and internet communications pursuant to secret court orders from the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Court (FISC). These orders compelled AT&T to hand over data on millions of users without individual warrants, justified under national security provisions. AT&T's cooperation involved installing surveillance equipment and daily data transfers to NSA facilities.
process_and_outcome: Court-approved orders were renewed periodically; public disclosure via leaks led to congressional oversight and reforms like the USA Freedom Act in 2015, limiting bulk collection. AT&T faced lawsuits but no direct fines for compliance.

Twitter's transparency report for 2014 detailed 2,289 U.S. government information requests, with compliance in 69% of cases after review. The company notified affected users where possible and litigated against secrecy orders.
process_and_outcome: Twitter provided data leading to account suspensions and criminal referrals. No adverse actions against Twitter; compliance aided counter-terrorism efforts.

Compliance involved providing access to customer content after verifying legal process, with notifications to customers unless prohibited. Examples include data from investigations into cybercrimes and fraud rings using AWS servers.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Data Access Fine
example_background: In 2023, Ireland's Data Protection Commission conducted an inquiry into Meta Platforms Ireland Limited's processing of personal data in the context of Facebook's 'pay or consent' model introduced in late 2021. The model required EU users to either pay a monthly fee for an ad-free experience or consent to data processing for personalized advertising. The inquiry examined compliance with GDPR requirements for data access, rectification, erasure, and objection to processing. The Commission found that Meta's mechanisms did not adequately provide users with rights to access their data or effectively object to processing for certain purposes, as the consent model bundled necessary processing with non-essential advertising data use, limiting user control over their information.
process_and_outcome: The DPC issued a decision fining Meta €1.2 billion for infringements related to international data transfers, but also addressed data subject rights issues. Meta was ordered to bring processing operations into compliance within five months and faced additional scrutiny on rights fulfillment. Meta appealed aspects of the decision.
source_rule: This includes the rights to request access or correct your information, request that your information be deleted, or object to or restrict GitHub from using it for certain purposes.
relevant_rules: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Articles 12-23 (data subject rights)

example_name: Marriott International GDPR Guest Data Fine
example_background: Marriott International acquired Starwood Hotels and Resorts in 2016 but did not fully integrate data protection measures until 2018, when a breach exposed passport numbers, payment card details, and other personal information of up to 383 million guests. The ICO's investigation extended to Marriott's post-breach response to data subject rights requests from affected individuals seeking access to their data, confirmation of breach details, deletion of compromised information, and objection to further processing. Marriott's systems were found lacking in timely and complete responses, with some requests taking months to process due to fragmented data storage across legacy systems.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million. Marriott was directed to overhaul its data subject rights handling processes, including appointing dedicated teams and automating responses. The company also faced class-action lawsuits in the US.
source_rule: This includes the rights to request access or correct your information, request that your information be deleted, or object to or restrict GitHub from using it for certain purposes.
relevant_rules: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Articles 15-21 (data subject rights)

example_background: In 2023, the UK's ICO investigated TikTok's handling of children's personal data, focusing on failures to uphold rights such as parental access requests, deletion of child accounts upon request, and restrictions on data use for profiling. TikTok's platform allowed children under 13 to create accounts without adequate age verification, and when deletion or access requests were made, the company often retained data or provided incomplete information due to decentralized data processing across global servers. The probe highlighted systemic issues in responding to rights exercises by parents and children.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined TikTok £12.7 million in 2023 for mishandling children's data, including rights violations. TikTok was mandated to enhance age-assurance tools and rights fulfillment mechanisms, with ongoing monitoring.
source_rule: This includes the rights to request access or correct your information, request that your information be deleted, or object to or restrict GitHub from using it for certain purposes.

example_name: Microsoft GDPR Data Subject Services Portal
example_background: Microsoft launched its GDPR Data Subject Requests (DSR) portal in 2018, enabling EU users of services like Office 365 and Azure to submit requests for data access, portability, correction, deletion, or objection directly. The portal handles requests end-to-end, providing downloadable data exports, status tracking, and automated fulfillment where possible. Microsoft reports fulfilling over 10 million DSRs annually, with average response times under 30 days, demonstrating scalable infrastructure for rights management.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft achieved full GDPR compliance certification and has not faced significant rights-related fines. The portal serves as a model in transparency reports submitted to regulators.
source_rule: This includes the rights to request access or correct your information, request that your information be deleted, or object to or restrict GitHub from using it for certain purposes.

example_name: Amazon GDPR Alexa Voice Data Rights Fine
example_background: In 2020, Luxembourg's CNPD fined Amazon for privacy violations related to Alexa voice recordings. Users requesting deletion of voice data or access to processed recordings faced challenges due to opaque retention practices and bundled consent for data use in AI training. Amazon's responses to objection requests for advertising purposes were incomplete, as data continued to be used across services without clear segmentation.
process_and_outcome: CNPD issued a €746 million fine, one of the largest under GDPR. Amazon enhanced its voice data dashboard for easier rights exercises and appealed the decision.

example_name: Clearview AI Data Subject Rights Lawsuit
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from the internet to build a facial recognition database sold to law enforcement. In 2022, the CNIL ordered Clearview to cease processing EU residents' data after complaints about ignored requests for data deletion and objection to biometric processing. Users whose images were in the database submitted rights requests that went unheeded, as Clearview lacked mechanisms to identify and remove specific individuals' data from its vast, unstructured dataset.
process_and_outcome: CNIL banned Clearview's operations in France and fined €20 million. Multiple EU authorities coordinated similar actions, leading to global scrutiny and lawsuits.
source_rule: This includes the rights to request access or correct your information, request that your information be deleted, or object to or restrict GitHub from using it for certain purposes.

example_background: Marriott International acquired Starwood Hotels in 2016, inheriting a customer database with personal information of about 383 million guests worldwide, including 300 million from Europe. The database contained names, phone numbers, travel details, payment card numbers, and passport information for some. Prior to full integration, hackers accessed the system undetected from 2014. Marriott notified authorities in 2018 after discovering the breach. Key issues included lack of explicit consent verification for data retention and sharing across the merged entities, and failure to ensure third-party (Starwood) data handling complied with consent requirements before processing.
process_and_outcome: The UK's Information Commissioner's Office fined Marriott £18.4 million (about $24 million) in 2020 under GDPR. The company appealed but later withdrew. Marriott settled class-action lawsuits in the US for $52 million and enhanced data protection measures globally.
source_rule: 1. With your consent (such to contact your references). It is your responsibility to obtain consent from references before providing their personal information to GitHub.

example_background: Apple introduced App Tracking Transparency (ATT) in iOS 14.5 in 2021, requiring apps to obtain explicit user consent before tracking them across apps and websites via the Identifier for Advertisers (IDFA). This feature ensures that developers and third parties cannot access personal data for advertising without user permission. It directly addresses consent for sharing device and behavioral data, analogous to reference consents, by prompting users with a clear opt-in dialog. Apple enforces this across its ecosystem, blocking non-compliant apps.
process_and_outcome: The policy has been upheld with no major regulatory fines against Apple. It influenced industry standards, with compliance leading to billions in ad revenue adjustments for Meta and Google. Apple continues to refine ATT based on user feedback.

example_background: Google's Android OS allowed apps to access Android ID and Advertising ID for tracking without always requiring explicit user consent prior to collection and sharing with third parties. CNIL found that pre-installed Google apps and the Play Store processed these identifiers for advertising purposes without proper consent banners or opt-ins, affecting hundreds of millions of users in the EU.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Google €150 million in 2022. Google implemented consent mode updates and integrated better prompts in Android settings.

A breach discovered in 2018 exposed data dating back to 2014, highlighting that Marriott had not implemented routine data purging or consent renewal processes for long-retained guest profiles, which were kept for marketing and loyalty program purposes far beyond transactional needs.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million (initially proposed at £99 million, reduced on appeal). Additional fines came from other regulators. Marriott was mandated to overhaul its data retention policies, conduct data inventories, and delete unnecessary personal data.

example_name: CNIL Fine Against Google for Data Retention
example_background: Google faced scrutiny from the French data protection authority (CNIL) over its handling of user personal data across Android devices and services. The investigation revealed that Google retained user location data, search histories, and advertising profiles indefinitely unless users manually opted out through complex settings. There were no default retention limits or automatic deletion mechanisms for candidate-like data from job applications via Google services or general user data, leading to accumulation of personal information over years without periodic consent refresh or purging.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €150 million fine in 2022 for GDPR violations related to data retention and consent. Google was ordered to implement time-bound retention policies and transparent deletion processes.

example_name: Apple GDPR Data Deletion Request Compliance
example_background: Following the implementation of the GDPR in May 2018, Apple received numerous data subject access requests (DSARs) from EU users seeking to access, rectify, or delete their personal data under Articles 15, 16, and 17. Apple established a dedicated online portal for users to submit requests directly, covering data such as iCloud backups, App Store purchase history, and device analytics. The company processed requests within the 30-90 day timelines mandated by GDPR, providing structured data exports and confirming deletions where applicable. This was part of Apple's broader privacy framework, including features like App Tracking Transparency introduced later.
process_and_outcome: Apple's processes were audited during GDPR enforcement periods with no major fines issued for DSAR handling. The company publicly reported handling millions of requests efficiently, contributing to its reputation for privacy leadership. Independent reviews by regulators confirmed adherence.
source_rule: You can make a request to do so by contacting privacy@github.com. We will respond to all requests in accordance with applicable data protection laws.

This tool was developed in response to GDPR and similar laws, enabling users to exercise rights without contacting support. Microsoft reports handling billions of data elements and processes requests at scale.
process_and_outcome: The dashboard has been praised in regulatory audits and transparency reports. No significant enforcement actions for DSAR non-compliance. Microsoft publishes annual transparency reports detailing request volumes and fulfillment rates.

example_background: In May 2023, the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) fined Meta's Facebook Ireland €1.2 billion for transferring personal data of EU users to the US without adequate safeguards following the EU-US Privacy Shield invalidation in 2020 by the Schrems II ruling. Meta relied on Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) deemed insufficient without supplementary measures to protect against US surveillance laws. The decision affected millions of users' data flows for services like News Feed and targeted ads.
process_and_outcome: The fine was confirmed after input from other EU DPAs via the European Data Protection Board (EDPB). Meta announced use of new EU-US Data Privacy Framework but faced ongoing scrutiny. The company was ordered to suspend transfers and bring existing data home within five months.
